video_title: Lawfare Daily: What Do the Russians Actually Think About the War?
transcript: [music] >> People's in Moldova support Russia's so-called special military operation and at the same time >> [music] >> they want peace. They want to stop the war, also the majority, right? How is this possible while the if the war keeps escalating, [music] right? It's because fundamentally the war is a disturbance. >> It's the Lawfare podcast. I'm Anastasia Lapathina, Ukraine fellow at Lawfare with Maria Snegovaya, a senior fellow for Russia and Eurasia at the Center for Strategic and International Studies. >> The big question is what the response will be if Russia would start losing territories. Probably it will happen against the backdrop [music] of the economic crisis, serious economic crisis where the preference would be just, you know, let it be within the group, right? This is when the probably there will be a really dramatic decline [music] for the war in general, for the idea of this special military operation. >> Maria and I sat down to discuss how Ukrainian attacks on Russian energy sites are changing Russian attitudes towards the war and what Russian polling can tell us about the war's future. Maria, you recently co-authored a report with Jade McGlynn for CSS called Russian attitudes are shifting as the war's effects come home. And so I invited you today to talk about this report and go a bit more in depth on some of its points. And then also talk about Russian domestic politics more broadly. And in your report, which is fascinating and we'll link it below in the description of the episode as always, you and your co-author link to a lot of various polling. >> [snorts] >> And I wanted to start with the sort of very broad question of how reliable is Russian domestic opinion polling and could you also tell us about the specific polling that you are using there? You're you're referencing a lot extreme scan in this report. So can you just talk a bit to lay the groundwork for the conversation? >> So, this is not my first engagement with this very controversial issue, especially among many of the Russian liberals. The key controversy since the start of the war has to do that based on the official data and the polling numbers, the support for the war, no matter how horrible and atrocious, has consistently remained high in Russia. And many authors therefore question the reliability of the polling in the third-turn context. So, full disclosure, I'm a political scientist, Columbia University trained. And in my analysis, I do work with polling data both in Russia and across Central Europe. The notion that analysts and researchers cannot work with polling in the third-turn settings by default is not correct. Even in China and even in Iran, there are polls being run and there are like peer-reviewed articles being published. Russia is not an exception. And frankly, before 2022, few people seriously questioned the reliability at least of the broad of trends that are being reported by the polls. So, it's important to keep in mind that many of the questions emerged as the 2022 war started. It just felt very unbelievable to many observers that Russians would, you know, rally around something as similarly unjustified, controversial, and atrocious as this war began. But in 2024, late 2024, specifically for the Atlantic Council, I made the analysis of the works by many of various pollsters, political scientists, who specifically tried to engage with that very question, whether polls in Russia are reliable. And the way they do this is very technical. It's And basically, for our audience, it's important to realize that for a researcher to find some inconsistencies would be big finding in such a, you know, contested political space, it would be great. So, many scholars would genuinely interested in finding some biases and they looked at the way the samples had been constructed, the various technical indicators, you know, how do people answer sensitive questions. There are a number of methodological tools to use like list experiments when you ask the question indirectly allowing the person an escape route, right? If they don't really want to publicly announce their dislike for the war. And all of them more or less consistently exposed that to the extent that you could trust the polling data before, you could trust it after. There are no major biases in the Russian polling. To the extent that such biases do exist, they probably remain at several percentage points one of the studies has found, for example, up to 10 percentage points using list experiment. The other ones that found like zero. So, it's within that number which by the way probably corresponds to the number of genuine liberals in Russia. Uh based on alternative sort of data by the Levada Center. So, all together to the extent that we trusted the Russian polling data before 2022, we can broadly trust it after 2022. Of course, it's a repressive environment. Of course, people also consume a lot of propaganda. So, all of that plays a role and we should anticipate that a much more free environment these numbers will change. But nonetheless, people are generally aligning with the state policies and that's side indications of it is the lack of strong anti-war movement for the last 4 and 1/2 years even outside of Russia where you can't speak up and you can be free like you can be like me and saying that the war is horrible on to an extent, right? There's a lot of There's a huge Russian diaspora. Of course, some have relatives in Russia, but others are more free to speak and many have escaped specifically to be able to tell the truth. Yet, we do not see this anti-war movement unfortunately broadly reflecting this conflicted attitude on the side of even Russians in exile to the war. >> So, if someone would ask you how many Russians support the war, what would you say? >> I said this passive alignment. I would say around the war, people not necessarily willing to bear any personal sacrifices has been fluctuating about 70% 70 to 80%. It depends on your I think 70% more reliable number. It depends on the method by which the questions are asked and it depends on the time. In recent years, this has been eroding. So, it's more in mid-60s now and you can also perhaps like factor in the again, this may be up to 10% points of those people who maybe maybe misrepresenting their views. So, all together still it's a majority and important to keep in mind that this is majority that includes both my ideological groups. We call them hawks in our analysis with Jade and more of the passive loyalists, you know, the who just just sort of follow along whatever the Kremlin does. They are a majority of this group and hence their opinions will change very quickly once the Kremlin changed its policies. >> Before we I do want to talk about those two groups. I think they're fascinating. But before we get into that, could you also explain extreme scan cuz you reference it a lot in your article and reference their numbers. >> After the war started and again in light of this controversy surrounding the Russian polling, many of the independent projects popped up and extreme scan is one of the examples of the independent projects by that not been funded by the state. It's run by Elena Koneva, prominent Russian sociologist, who by the way is recently also designated a foreign agent of Russia in Russia basically reflecting her independent posture. Uh the reason why we say that particular polling a lot, even if we do engage with others, right? In the wild Atlantic Council report, I did uh really go for absolute majority of the Russian pollsters, is that specifically extreme scan has uh looked at the war dynamic in Russian borderline regions, the one that are near the frontline with Ukraine, like Belgorod, Kursk. And uh we just argued with Jade that uh if you want to see at how the people respond to, you know, being immediate exposed to immediate experience of the war, uh arguably that's one kind of that's one way to look at how they will respond to the ongoing uh drone strikes at Russian refineries by Ukraine. So, that just looked like a more feasible comparison. >> Okay. So, with that out of the way, let's talk about the hawks and the loyalists. You you split the Russian society sort of in these two groups. So, who are they and how do they differ? >> So, it's important to keep in mind that while majority in Russia do, unfortunately, broadly align with the Kremlin's war, this support is not uniform. It's heterogeneous, as I mentioned. So, it is composed of a more hawkish ideological core. These are like broadly uh more likely a male from maybe a smaller towns, villages, who are actively consuming the state run as Kremlin propaganda on state TVs, largely perhaps influenced by the Soviet uh shaped leanings, and they truly believe in the importance of the strong impulse of Russia. Uh strong national identification is something that distinguishes them. And depending on the specific approach that you use, there's from 15 to 30%. It depends, right? The strength of the national identity can be used as selecting criteria in order to identify these groups, but specifically how many of them depends on basically the criteria that you impose. Important to keep in mind that they're a minority within this broader support for the war. And uh the kind of paradox is that for most of Putin's rule, this group tends to believe that Putin has been weak politically internationally, right? He does not adopt a strong enough posture uh to respond to the West to whatever they see as, you know, very Russia being unfairly treated on the international stage. A lot of revanchist sentiments associated to this group. So, they tend to rally behind Putin primarily when he starts the war. And that's one of the reasons why if you look at historically the polls, this is the fifth war Putin has started during his reign. And pretty much every single war was followed by this pattern of a spike in approval for both Putin and the war support rallying around the flag primarily because these hawks join in the support. So, they typically think that Putin is not strong enough, but finally once he starts the war, they believe they finally did the right thing. He came to senses. So, so-called Z-niks. Z-niks, Z-niks. >> And what percentage of the overall population is that? >> So, as I mentioned, it depends on the approach from 15 to 30%. It depends on the choice like how how you look at the strength of national identification. The important point is that they are a minority within this larger support group. And the much more important group and the one where like the whole controversy about the war support comes from in the first place, it's this loyalist group, moderate group. Although it's hard to call them moderate obviously when they actually support the war. But they are majority from again it varies from 35 to 40, some in some service up to 50%. And the key feature of this group, first of all, they're more likely to be female, maybe and don't care much about politics, right? They don't really like engage that very much with the political questions. Uh their media consumption is much more diverse. In general, they just want to, you know, live their life. And they broadly support everything that the Kremlin does. It's the status quo group. Russian opposition leaders who ran for elections back back in the day often complained that every time they tried to make um the like Russian electors to vote for them, they often hear that you have to be become the authority first, the power first, to get into power first, and then we'll vote vote for you. So, that's the important logic that people largely rally around whoever is in power, whatever the power represents today. As long as Putin is power, they're with it, and as long as they're more or less satisfied with the status quo. >> So, is this like your average rich Muscovite? >> I would say that perhaps you can argue, especially more recently, Moscow, unfortunately, has become very pro-Putin and pro-war. This has not been the case before. >> Uh there's a lot of uh debate about it. They really sort of redistributed uh Moscow added uh new provincial regions to basically what Moscow constitutes to Moscow's uh district. And uh also, just as you mentioned yourself, Moscow and many people in it actually benefited from the war, from the sanctions, or conventions. And uh as we will discuss later, the war support is very strongly associated to personal uh perception of economic well-being. Which is why it's so important what Ukraine is doing right now against the refineries. But uh broadly speaking, uh Moscow is doing pretty well and not suffering very much from the consequences of the war. And also, play like why the Russian liberals left, fled from the country, right? Many of the people who you may have met at Bolotnaya protests in 2012, for example, are currently in exile. They those were basically replaced with more uh pro-loyalist groups. It's also important to keep in mind that since 2011-12, the Kremlin deliberately uh kind of substituted the more independent entrepreneurs, businessmen types of jobs with uh the state public-related positions. So, that was actually deliberate policy to create an electorate that's more dependent on the state. So, a lot of jobs right now available in the primarily public sector, state-related sector. It's also associated with the ongoing renationalization. And as people becoming uh more dependent on the state, almost by default, right, they become more loyalist. And so, the Kremlin is sort of is quite aware of all these patterns and is very shrewd at sustaining its constituency that's largely dependent on the state support. >> So, you said that uh there are about 15 to 30% hawks, depending on how you count, yeah, how you define that. Uh what about the loyalists? >> So, the loyalists constitute about 35 to 50%. Again, it depends on your uh definition. The important thing is that they are a majority, right? And the large controversies surrounding Russian polling, so do they really support the war? Largely pertains to how we interpret the support of this group. Because uh you'll see in the Russian polling this controversy that shows that people simultaneously support Russia's so-called special military operation and at the same time they want peace, they want uh to stop the war, also the majority, right? How is this possible? >> Mhm. While the if the war keeps escalating, right? >> It's because fundamentally the war is a disturbance, right? That's not something necessarily that would be have been their first choice. But it's also something that the Kremlin has assigned has decided to do. And because often in say in focus groups, they say like I'm not personally a foreign policy expert, so I delegate these decisions for Putin. Putin does the right thing. If he decided to do it, he knows what he's done. So, basically they this delegation of the authority of agency if you will, creates this perception that whatever the state is doing is right as long as I'm fine. Like my life more or less continues as before and things are okay. So, the big really the big really interesting question is what's going to happen to them when they realize their life is no longer okay. And this is what we can discuss later. I will flag one thing. Even if the support is passive, even if a person says, "Okay, let Putin do what he wants as long as I'm fine and he does not bother me, right? I don't have to or my my son, my my husband doesn't have to go to the war, right? And I can just sustain my perfect little world as is." So, to what extent is this really a support? That's the big debate, right? Among especially people who question the reliability of polls and how we interpret this. But I will flag that fundamentally it is a green light, right? They they do not oppose what the Kremlin is doing and fundamentally allow the Kremlin to continue whatever it wants, no matter how atrocious horrible the war is and basically delegates the agency the authority to the Kremlin, right? That delegation is a choice, I would flag and accordingly that is still a support even if passive and latent. It allows the Kremlin or Putin to continue to do whatever they want. >> Do we know how much these people have access to actual reliable real information and news? I don't know if that's an impossible question to answer, but >> That's a fascinating question Anastasia and that's because of course absolutely we know that despite the fact that people increasingly get information on internet in Russia, even there you can just compare on Telegram the following of the liberal channels and oppositional minded channels and the so called Z pro war channels and Z channels and pro war channel Kremlin propaganda channels like by several orders of magnitude typically surpass the oppositional independent channels. That is definitely the case. The the TV remains the main source of news still, even if people increasingly shift to the internet. And even on internet, they seek out deliberately information that does not question their views, right? They to avoid so-called cognitive dissonance. This is one of the major shocks that we've discovered since 2022 that no matter how huge, seemingly, the development disruption, people will try to preserve like the world. They'll try to seek out the information that does not fundamentally challenge what they used to, what they feel comfortable with. Except when it concerns them personally. And this is again the fundamental challenge pertaining specifically to the group of loyalists. We see consistently, and we actually mentioned it with Jade, that once the shocks unravel, and that could be Prigozhin's attempted mutiny, or sanctions imposed by the West, it can be the ongoing drone strikes by Ukraine on Russian refineries. So, the moment something is not right, something like imbalances the perfect comfortable world in which they live, this is when people start seeking out actively independent information and following of the independent channels channels spikes along with anxiety levels in Russia. Every single time we again watch it right now, and you can talk to again the people the influences who run the independent channels, and they see this pattern. So, the paradox here that is that many people do know where to find more independent information, but they don't want to find it when things are fine. They only look up for it when things are not good for them. >> You say in your report that loyalist dissatisfaction tends to express itself as withdrawal, cynicism, private grumbling, and exit. Uh so, very much a very passive position. Why is that? >> Uh that's just what more natural for them, right? The fundamental lack of agency. The people don't really think in terms of agency, in terms of the ability to influence. >> That's a very Russian concept, right? I don't know if I'm running the risk of being too biased here, uh because I am based in Kyiv. So, [laughter] this is very complicated for me. >> Unfortunately, exactly what got Russia into this horrible place where it's now. Almost suicidal in some ways, right? Because of war that's horrible, that's not good for anybody. Horrible for Ukraine, but also horrible for Russia. But, uh this precisely the attitude that the things will be the Kremlin knows right, and I just mind my own business, and I just delegate the authority. The inability in some ways to think of oneself as having the agency, and broad perception of the power of the authorities, right? As weather. So, you know, when it rains, you don't try to stop the rain, you just seek a shelter, you get yourself an umbrella. That's the basic attitude. And if you look at uh one of the major shocks uh during this war, the announcement of this partial mobilization by Putin in uh the fall 2022, that's exactly the response that we've seen, right? How did people uh respond? People are not happy, we see that in the polling, Putin's approval and the war support dropped quite dramatically. And what we've seen is a huge exodus of people who tried to flee uh this mobilization from Russia. But, we did not see an attempted protest. At least mostly not. And there was some at effort in some regions of Caucasus in particular, but in general, the major response was avoidance. Having said that, you know, from Ukrainian perspective, this is just something to factor in, but fundamentally it's not necessarily nothing, right? If you cannot incentivize avoidance, sabotage, as we've seen in the late Soviet Union, as you mentioned, Anastasia, right? Very typical Soviet Russian response. That's still good enough, right? It's not It's not nothing. We can As long as people do not participate, maybe do not build those missiles, you know, do not go fight against Ukraine, that's good enough as long as that is possible to incentivize that behavior. But unfortunately, thinking about the Russia of the future, right? That sort of attitude is precisely what prevent Russia from democratizing because people just don't think of themselves as active citizens and don't try to take responsibility for what's going on with the country. And this is one of the reasons why since the start of the war, the debate of Russia's responsibility for the war has been so active, right? It's It's not an issue for most outside observers. Obviously, you're responsible, like I'm responsible, right? For what you my country is doing. It's unfortunately loaded by default by my very nationality. But that is a huge question mark for many in Russia because they just don't feel like they have anything to do with whatever the Kremlin is doing very often. Or as is more typical of the loyalist, they like, you know, the Kremlin knows best. I'm just a small person. Uh if Putin decided it, he's a smart guy. He knows what he's doing. If it wasn't for his actions, NATO would have been uh next to Moscow or whatever, an excuse they come up with. It's important, by the way, there's still no clear agreement as to why the war is being fought. The The most brutal war in Russia after Second World War, with the enormous number of casualties, still no clear justification on the authorities' side as to why the war is being fought and no clear agreement among the Russians. If you look at the justifications, not a single one, whether they're saving the Russian speakers in Ukraine, and , or whether they're trying to stop NATO from expanding, not a single justification got gathers over a 50% response. So, the people are quite divided on it. And yet, they still sort of uh accepting it. And that's the fundamental tragedy of Russia, the one that I'm arguing probably going to get it into some horrible, horrible place. >> You differentiate between military shocks and economic shocks. You've already touched on a little bit about this. So, tell me, how do they impact Russian public opinion differently? >> Yeah, and that's a fundamental contribution of our analysis with Jade. So, we start with the premise that there's two groups of Russians constituting this war support, the so-called hawks and the so-called loyalists, and the key reasons why it's important to think of Russian war support as heterogeneous is that they're going to respond differently to different sort of shocks. So, the key response from hawks, and and this is when we look at the patterns in the borderline regions of Russia uh citing extreme scant polling, is that hawks tend to rally around the flag because they think the war is justified and right, right by default, uh it's the right thing, and so the natural response is rallying around the flag. But, for the loyalists who already a little bit sort of on the defensive, you know, Putin knows what he's doing, but maybe, you know, I would be fine without the war, you know, I have other things to worry about, and in general we want the key preference is to improve the economic situation. For them, uh the shocks generally are problematic. We see, as I mentioned, consistent decline in war approval and Putin's approval associated to these major shocks. And particularly problematic for them is the economic impact of the war. Uh so, they are not willing to sacrifice uh their finances, their well-being, unlike the hawks, for some unclear purposes of this war because for them, as we discussed, there's no clear purpose in the first place. Putin doing this for whatever reason, he knows why he's doing it, but I don't really care. I I have other things to mind. And that is why economic shocks in particular so problematic for these groups, and uh we consistently see that the Russian economic decline as well as personal economic uh decline perception that my personal uh my finances are going to get worse is consistently associated to diminishing war support since early uh 2026, we've seen that Russian economy has been stagnating. Some analysts are even suggesting uh even a recession. And accordingly, we've seen the erosion of the war support and growing demand for peace talks together with Jade West Side a lot of these numbers. And more specifically, the attacks on the refineries that Ukraine is conducting therefore are very very instrumental. Of course, some will rally around the flag. We do actually see there's interviews with some people who can't find any gasoline in Russia, but they're like, but that's because we have not responded to Ukraine strong enough. That's their conclusion, right? But the majority are not happy. It's very clear. The crisis is spreading. And economically, this is all impacting people when already they know things are not going so well in Russia. I will flag that this is the relatively new development. In the first couple of years of the war, Putin implemented this so-called military Keynesianism policies, right? They a lot of money has been in influxed into the economy, and as a result, there was actually an increase in people's economic well-being. Equality declined, and especially for working class groups who benefited also from this competition for the jobs, for the military industrial complex, the the well-being has improved, and so that actually coincided with growing support for war in the first couple of the years, but that is done now. The economy is in decline, and on top of that, you have this issue with gasoline that affects pretty much everyone. This is a something that Ukraine has found that really is important for Russians, right? Everybody experiences this. Shortages of the gasoline, and these lines we also flag with Jade that generally signify this reminiscent of the late Soviet period when people were in lines and things were not great in the country, and now suddenly this is back. >> It's a very typical image for anyone growing up or living in the space. Yes, the lines for anything is very dramatic. >> Yeah, even in America, I always find myself surprised as people willing to stay in line. Primarily affecting the loyalists, these groups for whom as I mentioned, the economic considerations matter far more than any ideological considerations. And that directly coincides with ongoing decline in war approval. We sign one of the polling companies that reports the Institute of Conflictology and Analysis of Russia Ecar polling that for the first time since the start of the war has found a record 81% of respondents say that they want the war to end. Senses. But I think that to the extent that that point exists it will take an economic crisis. A full blown economic of the kind right maybe seen in 2008 or like late Soviet Union and most likely gradual, not not fast, right? The one of the things that we've flagged with Jade is that while the attacks on refineries are definitely impacting the Russian opinion as we discussed, right? It will take quite some time. It's a gradual change and the propaganda and there's a lot of forces within the Kremlin that has a much better grip on the Russian public sentiment and a lot of familiarity with it over the last 25 years, right? That works in the opposite direction. So, it's like various streams that are clashing and so it's not going to be as easy as one would imagine. Like Russian tolerance to casualties has just apparently been much, much stronger than many have expected, all right? Just a reminder that our CSIS colleagues recently released a report flagging this enormous attrition rate for the Russians, right? That this war has exceeded all of the wars that Russia and the Soviet Union have fought since the end of the Second World War in casualties and yet they still keep coming and keep pushing for no clear purpose and without clear understanding why the war is being fought as we discussed. So, RAND Corporation just released an interesting analysis of studied dissertation that shows again that for Russians casualties in the war don't seem to be to play an important role even like on the, you know, ideational level. They, for example, if they offer to replace the people with robots, they don't necessarily, you know, care as much about few fewer human losses, fewer battlefield losses. What it matters more apparently is the war is morally justified and they're likely to succeed, which is somewhat paradoxical given that we don't even know what Georgia you even doesn't even agree why the war is being fought and whether it's morally justified. But just something to keep in mind. We have discovered that the price of human life in Russia is extremely low. We kind of knew that already, but it's also extremely visible now for after 4 and 1/2 years of this war. But the economic cost is where many of the loyalists want to sort of ride along, who are not necessarily like super ideologically invested in this war, are going to start increasingly questioning this developments and that is already happening. It has to be happening at large scale and it's very important that 4 and 1/2 years after the war started, the war is going in a completely opposite direction from what the Kremlin has imagined it to be, right? It's not not at all an occupation of Ukraine in 3 days or weeks. In fact, the war is coming back to Russia and Ukraine is stronger and stronger and much more sophisticated and and adapted at influencing Russia. And last but not the least, I think that the economic cost matters beyond just shifting public opinion, right? As I mentioned before, we have not not not a complete understanding of how Russians are responding to the war and the Kremlin is very good at normalizing so we together with JIT we've been showing this draft of our analysis to sociological colleagues and while most agreed with us, some have said that yes, but it's also possible that the Kremlin will manage to present this gasoline crisis as a new normal and it's also possible that people will agree because we've seen unfortunately repeatedly that being the case in Russia even where it seems as an outsider it seems completely paradoxical and shocking that people will accept it. But the second reason why the war is important is that just the economic cost is important is that they just shrink the resource at the Kremlin's disposal, right? Less gasoline available is not just less gasoline for Russians, it's just less gasoline also for the Russian army and this is a fundamental issue. Putin with fewer resources and much weaker Putin hence capable of uh way fewer less destruction for Ukraine. So, that is another channel through which this impact is working. >> I mean, also uh just to follow up, A, does Putin even care about public opinion? And B, does he care about it considering that we discussed that even if loyalists uh you know, start losing faith in what the Kremlin is doing, their reaction is inward. So, we're not going to see any sort of public revolution or public protest. >> This infection will spread. And we know from my multiple political science footage is that public dissatisfaction is more likely in autocratic regimes to lead to elite splits, right? So, it's not an instant process, but it's not nothing. And the Kremlin does care about the public opinion. We know that they do multiple polling, right? They do worry a lot about the Russian identity, how the Russians think of themselves, and they know that's a really powerful tool to mobilize people to for the war. So, that is for sure. And uh we know that public opinion to an extent matters because to date the Kremlin tried to create this perception of normalcy in Russia, right? If you remember the partial mobilization, there was multiple pro-Kremlin ads, influencers, influencing campaigns trying to demonstrate that share of people who are being called, uh recruited is really minor, and nobody has something to worry about. And that's consistently the way the Kremlin chose to present the war to Russians. Don't worry, look elsewhere, things are fine, everything's great, we're winning, just give us some time, mind your own business. And uh that's got to be done for a reason, right? That's probably because they know uh that the state is less responsive to alternatives. And uh as we discussed, even if these are not the main considerations that will uh alter Putin's perceptions, nonetheless it will make it harder for the Kremlin to fight this war, create more obstacles in the situation of already shrinking economic resources, more challenging trade-offs. So, while the public opinion is by far not the main reasons why I think the Kremlin is fighting the war, it does play a role in either enabling or preventing it uh the Kremlin from fully investing in this war effort, and accordingly, it matters for Ukraine. >> Well, Maria, this was fascinating. Thank you so much for coming on, and we'll have you on again in the future. >> Thanks a lot for having me. >> The Lawfare Podcast is produced by the Lawfare Institute. If you want to support the show and listen ad free, you can become a Lawfare material supporter at lawfaremedia.org/support. Supporters also get access to special events and other bonus content we don't share anywhere else. If you enjoy the podcast, please rate and review us wherever you listen. It really does help. And be sure to check out our new other shows, including Rational Security, Scaling Laws, Allies, The Aftermath, and Escalation. [music] Our latest Lawfare Presents podcast series about the war in Ukraine. You can also find all of our written work at lawfaremedia.org. The podcast is edited by Jen Patja. Our theme song is from Alabama Music. As always, thanks for [music] listening.

For them, uh the shocks generally are
